items.name: Haemoglobin
items.tip: Always follow Hb with MCV to classify. Hb <8 g/dL = consider transfusion threshold. Symptoms matter — not just the number. Haemodilution in pregnancy lowers Hb (normal ≥10.5 in 3rd trimester).

items.name: MCV (Mean Cell Volume)
items.tip: Mnemonic MALT for microcytic. Normocytic = haemolysis, bleeding, renal disease, early deficiency. MCV alone is not enough — always pair with RDW and clinical context.

items.name: MCH (Mean Cell Haemoglobin)
items.tip: MCH closely mirrors MCV. Low MCH + low MCV = classic iron deficiency hypochromic picture. MCH alone rarely changes management but confirms the MCV pattern.

items.name: MCHC (Mean Cell Hb Concentration)
items.tip: MCHC >36 strongly suggests hereditary spherocytosis — the only common cause. Do osmotic fragility test and blood film. Low MCHC = hypochromic = iron deficiency until proven otherwise.

items.name: RDW (Red Cell Distribution Width)
items.tip: KEY differentiator: Iron deficiency = HIGH RDW. Thalassaemia trait = NORMAL RDW with low MCV. RDW rises before MCV drops in early iron deficiency — it's the earliest CBC marker of iron deficiency. Use it!

items.name: WBC (White Blood Cells)
items.tip: WBC alone is insufficient — always request DIFFERENTIAL. Neutrophils = bacterial. Lymphocytes = viral. Eosinophils = NAACP. Monocytes = TB/fungal/chronic. Basophils = CML.

items.name: Neutrophils
items.tip: <0.5 = severe neutropenia — high infection risk, reverse barrier nursing. Left shift (bands / metamyelocytes on film) = active infection. Clozapine: mandatory WBC monitoring. Agranulocytosis = stop drug immediately.

items.name: Lymphocytes
items.tip: Atypical lymphocytes on film = EBV (mono). Smear cells = CLL. CD4 <200 = AIDS-defining illness. Lymphocytosis + B symptoms + lymphadenopathy = rule out lymphoma urgently.

items.name: Eosinophils
items.tip: NAACP mnemonic. >0.5 = eosinophilia requiring investigation. >1.5 = hypereosinophilic syndrome — organ damage risk (cardiac, pulmonary, neurological). Eosinophilia + asthma + vasculitis = EGPA (Churg-Strauss).

items.name: Platelets
items.tip: <50 = significant bleeding risk. <20 = spontaneous bleed risk. HIT: platelet drop >50% on heparin + thrombosis = STOP heparin, use alternative anticoagulant. TTP = schistocytes + thrombocytopenia + neurological + renal failure + fever.

items.name: Reticulocytes
items.tip: High retics + anaemia = marrow working but losing RBCs (haemolysis/bleeding). Low retics + anaemia = marrow not responding (aplasia/deficiency). Corrected reticulocyte index <2% = inadequate response = bone marrow problem.

items.name: ESR
items.tip: ESR >100 = myeloma, malignancy, serious infection (TB, endocarditis), vasculitis until proven otherwise. Combine with CRP: both ↑ = active inflammation. ESR ↑ + CRP normal = consider myeloma (paraprotein levels, SPEP).

items.name: CRP (C-Reactive Protein)
up: Bacterial infection ★ (rises within 4–6 hrs, peaks 24–72 hrs)
Autoimmune flares (except SLE — see hsCRP card)
dn: SLE flare (paradoxically LOW — use ESR instead) ★
items.tip: CRP rises and falls FASTER than ESR — the best marker for monitoring treatment response. CRP >100 = bacterial infection or major tissue injury. CRP 5–50 = mild-moderate inflammation, non-specific. Low CRP in suspected SLE flare actually SUPPORTS the diagnosis (SLE doesn't raise CRP unless serositis or secondary infection). Serial CRP every 24–48 hrs is more useful than a single value.

items.name: hsCRP (High-Sensitivity CRP)
items.ref: Low CV risk: <1.0 mg/L   |   Intermediate: 1.0–3.0 mg/L   |   High CV risk: >3.0 mg/L   |   Acute inflammation: >10 mg/L (invalidates CV risk use)
up: Cardiovascular disease risk ★ (chronic low-grade atherosclerotic inflammation)
Any acute infection or inflammation (spikes >10 mg/L — cannot interpret for CV risk)
dn: Statins ★ (reduce hsCRP independent of LDL — JUPITER trial 2008)
items.tip: hsCRP and standard CRP measure the SAME molecule — difference is only assay SENSITIVITY. Standard CRP detects ≥5 mg/L (infection/inflammation). hsCRP detects 0.1–10 mg/L (cardiovascular risk). FOUR KEY RULES: (1) Use hsCRP for 10-year CVD risk stratification alongside lipids and BP — not for diagnosing infection. (2) If hsCRP >10 mg/L: acute infection/inflammation is present — CANNOT interpret for CV risk, repeat in 2–3 weeks after recovery. (3) JUPITER trial (Ridker 2008): rosuvastatin in patients with LDL <3.4 mmol/L BUT hsCRP >2 mg/L reduced MI/stroke by 44% — established hsCRP as statin therapy target even with normal LDL. (4) hsCRP reflects chronic low-grade inflammation driven by diet, stress, obesity, and physical inactivity — rises years before clinical cardiovascular events. Reynolds Risk Score includes hsCRP for women's CVD risk. Always order on a healthy, infection-free day for CV risk assessment.

items.name: ALT (Alanine Aminotransferase)
items.tip: ALT is MORE liver-specific than AST. ALT > AST = viral/NAFLD. AST:ALT ≥2:1 = alcoholic hepatitis. ALT >1000 = ischaemic hepatitis, acute viral, or paracetamol toxicity. Always compare to baseline trend.

items.name: AST (Aspartate Aminotransferase)
items.tip: AST:ALT ≥2:1 = alcoholic hepatitis (De Ritis ratio). Isolated AST rise: check CK — likely muscle source, not liver. Less liver-specific than ALT. Troponin more specific for MI than AST.

items.name: ALP (Alkaline Phosphatase)
items.tip: Isolated ALP: check GGT. GGT ↑ = liver origin. GGT normal = BONE origin (Paget's, bone mets). ALP + GGT + bilirubin ↑ = cholestatic pattern. Pregnancy ALP = placental isoenzyme — completely physiological, don't investigate.

items.name: GGT (Gamma-Glutamyl Transferase)
items.tip: GGT is the ALCOHOL marker — rises with regular drinking even before liver damage. Confirms liver origin of raised ALP. Normalises 2–6 weeks after stopping alcohol. Very sensitive but non-specific — elevated in many liver conditions.

items.name: Bilirubin (Total)
items.tip: Visible jaundice >35 µmol/L. Unconjugated ↑ = pre-hepatic (haemolysis) or Gilbert's — urine bilirubin ABSENT. Conjugated ↑ = hepatic or post-hepatic — bilirubin appears in urine (dark urine). Always check direct/indirect split.

items.name: Albumin
items.tip: Half-life ~20 days — reflects CHRONIC synthetic function. Acute illness lowers albumin non-specifically (negative APP). Used in Child-Pugh score. Check urine ACR if albumin low — could be nephrotic syndrome.

items.name: PT / INR
up: Liver failure ★ (factors II, VII, IX, X ↓)
items.tip: BEST acute synthetic function marker (factor VII t½ = 6 hrs — responds within hours). Prolonged PT that CORRECTS with Vit K = Vit K deficiency (fat malabsorption). Does NOT correct = liver failure. Critical in acute liver failure — drives transplant listing (King's College Criteria).

items.name: LDH (Lactate Dehydrogenase)
items.tip: Non-specific but essential. Haemolysis triad: ↑LDH + ↑unconjugated bilirubin + ↓haptoglobin. Very high LDH in lymphoma = adverse prognosis (IPI score). Also elevated in PE — non-specific marker.

items.name: Haptoglobin
items.tip: Low haptoglobin = haemolysis until proven otherwise. Consumed binding free Hb in intravascular haemolysis. Interpret with LDH + bilirubin. Absent in neonates — not useful in newborns. Normal haptoglobin does NOT exclude extravascular haemolysis.

items.name: Total Protein
items.tip: Total protein = albumin + globulins. High total protein + low albumin = high globulins → send SPEP/SIFE + serum free light chains (myeloma screen). Globulin gap >4 g/dL is abnormal — always investigate.

items.name: Creatinine
up: Drugs: cimetidine, trimethoprim (block secretion — not true renal impairment)
dn: Low muscle mass (elderly, cachexia, limb amputation)
items.tip: Does NOT rise until ~50% nephron loss. Always compare to BASELINE. Trend > single value. Muscle-dependent — a fit 25-year-old male with creatinine 115 is very different from a frail 80-year-old woman. Always check eGFR.

items.name: Urea (BUN)
up: Upper GI bleeding ★ (digested blood = protein load)
items.tip: Urea:Creatinine ratio (µmol:µmol) >100:1 = pre-renal or UPPER GI BLEED. <100:1 = intrinsic or post-renal. Low urea + raised creatinine = liver failure context. In GI bleed: Urea rises disproportionately to creatinine.

items.name: eGFR
dn: False elevation: low muscle mass (high eGFR despite poor renal function)
items.tip: CKD-EPI accounts for age, sex, race, creatinine. Use for CKD STAGING. Drug dose adjustments: metformin stop at G3b; many antibiotics (gentamicin, vancomycin), anticoagulants need adjustment at G3+. eGFR unreliable in AKI — use creatinine trend instead.

items.name: Sodium (Na⁺)
items.tip: ASSESS VOLUME STATUS FIRST. Hypovolaemic = diuretics/Addison's. Euvolaemic = SIADH. Hypervolaemic = CCF/cirrhosis/nephrotic. Correct Na slowly (<10 mmol/L per 24 hrs) to avoid central pontine myelinolysis (osmotic demyelination).

items.name: Potassium (K⁺)
items.tip: K+ >6.5 = CARDIAC EMERGENCY. ECG: peaked T → PR prolongation → wide QRS → sine wave → VF. TREAT: Calcium gluconate FIRST (membrane stabilisation, 10 mins), then insulin+dextrose, salbutamol neb, sodium bicarbonate if acidotic. Dialysis if severe or persistent.

items.name: Bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻)
items.tip: Low HCO₃ → calculate ANION GAP: Na - (Cl + HCO₃). Normal AG = 8–12. High AG (>12) = MUDPILES. Normal AG acidosis = RTA, diarrhoea, saline excess (hyperchloraemic). Use delta-delta ratio to check if mixed disorder.

items.name: Phosphate (PO₄³⁻)
dn: DKA treatment (insulin drives Pi into cells)
items.tip: Refeeding syndrome: severe hypophosphataemia after refeeding a malnourished patient — can cause cardiac arrest and respiratory failure. Monitor electrolytes closely in all refeeding protocols. CKD: high phosphate = vascular calcification risk → dietary restriction + binders.

items.name: Calcium (Corrected Ca²⁺)
items.tip: CORRECT for albumin: Adjusted Ca = measured Ca + 0.02 × (40 − albumin). Symptoms: Bones-Groans-Stones-Psychic moans. Ca >3.5 = crisis: rehydrate IV + bisphosphonate. Check PTH to differentiate primary HPT from malignancy (PTH suppressed in malignancy).

items.name: Uric Acid (Urate)
items.tip: Single raised urate does NOT diagnose gout — need clinical features or synovial fluid (negatively birefringent needle crystals under polarised light). Tumour lysis syndrome: rising K + PO₄ + urate + LDH + falling Ca = emergency — rasburicase + aggressive hydration.

items.name: Serum Osmolality
items.tip: Osmolar gap = measured − calculated [2×Na + glucose + urea]. Gap >10 = toxic alcohol (methanol, ethylene glycol, ethanol, propylene glycol). Essential in any unexplained high AG metabolic acidosis. Also calculate to confirm hyperglycaemia as cause of hyperosmolality.

items.name: Chloride (Cl⁻)
items.tip: Essential for anion gap calculation. Hyperchloraemia + low HCO₃ + normal AG = hyperchloraemic metabolic acidosis → think RTA or diarrhoea or excess saline. Hypochloraemia + high HCO₃ = metabolic alkalosis — think vomiting or diuretics.

items.name: Protein (Dipstick / ACR)
items.tip: ACR >3 mg/mmol = microalbuminuria (early diabetic nephropathy). ACR >30 = macroalbuminuria. Dipstick misses Bence Jones protein — send urine electrophoresis if myeloma suspected. Transient proteinuria: fever, exercise, CCF. Repeat if unexpected positive.

items.name: Blood (Haematuria)
items.tip: ALWAYS send for MC&S AND cytology if unexpected haematuria. RBC casts = glomerulonephritis (nephritic syndrome). Persistent unexplained haematuria in >40yrs = urology referral (2-week wait, rule out malignancy). Dipstick detects haemoglobin, myoglobin, and free Hb — microscopy needed to confirm RBCs.

items.name: Leucocytes & Nitrites
up: Leucocytes: UTI ★, pyuria, catheter, contamination, interstitial nephritis
items.tip: Nitrites negative does NOT exclude UTI — Gram-positive organisms (Enterococcus, Staph) don't produce nitrites. Leucocytes alone = non-specific. Sterile pyuria = TB ★, interstitial nephritis, urethritis (chlamydia). Both positive + symptoms = treat as UTI without waiting for culture.

items.name: Glucose (Glucosuria)
up: Diabetes mellitus ★ (blood glucose >10 mmol/L = renal threshold)
items.tip: Glucosuria = blood glucose exceeded renal threshold (normally ~10 mmol/L). Normal urine glucose DOES NOT exclude diabetes — threshold varies. Glucosuria with normal blood glucose = renal tubular defect (Fanconi syndrome — also aminoaciduria, phosphaturia).

items.name: Ketones
up: Type 1 DM: sick day rules — always check
items.tip: In suspected DKA: check urine ketones AND serum beta-hydroxybutyrate (more sensitive/specific). Urine ketones are acetoacetate — serum is BHB. Euglycaemic DKA (SGLT2 inhibitors, pregnancy): ketones elevated with near-normal glucose — easy to miss!

items.name: Bilirubin & Urobilinogen
up: Urobilinogen ↑: haemolysis ★, hepatocellular disease, early obstruction
dn: Urobilinogen absent: complete biliary obstruction (pale stool + dark urine)
items.tip: Urine bilirubin present = conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia — liver disease or obstruction. Absent bilirubin + high urobilinogen = pre-hepatic jaundice (haemolysis, Gilbert's). Absent both = complete obstruction. Use in conjunction with serum bilirubin fractions.

items.name: pH & Specific Gravity
up: High pH (alkaline) >8: UTI (urea-splitting organisms — Proteus), RTA type 1, vegetarian diet, post-meal
High SG >1.030: dehydration, SIADH, glycosuria, proteinuria
dn: Low pH (acid) <4.5: metabolic acidosis, high protein diet, ketoacidosis
Low SG <1.005: DI ★, psychogenic polydipsia, overhydration
items.tip: SG reflects renal concentrating ability. Fixed SG ~1.010 = isosthenuria = severely impaired concentrating ability (CKD, ATN). Inability to concentrate urine (SG <1.010 despite dehydration) = DI or tubular disease. Urine pH >7 in acidosis = RTA (kidney can't acidify).

items.name: ACR & PCR (Quantitative Proteinuria)
up: ACR 3–30: Microalbuminuria (early diabetic nephropathy, cardiovascular risk)
items.tip: ACR preferred over 24-hr urine collection — equally accurate, far more practical. ACR >3 in diabetics = start ACE-i/ARB regardless of BP. Rising ACR in CKD = poor prognosis. Nephrotic syndrome: oedema + hypoalbuminaemia + hyperlipidaemia + massive proteinuria (>3.5g/24hr).

items.name: Urine MC&S (Microscopy, Culture & Sensitivity)
up: Most common: E.coli ★, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas, Enterococcus, Proteus
items.tip: Urine casts are formed in renal tubules — always pathological except hyaline casts. RBC casts = active glomerular disease (IgA nephropathy, post-streptococcal GN, vasculitis). Contaminated MSU: >2 organisms, squamous cells on microscopy. Always collect midstream clean-catch sample.

items.name: Total Cholesterol
items.tip: Total cholesterol alone is insufficient — always interpret with full lipid profile. Non-HDL cholesterol (TC − HDL) is a better CV risk predictor than LDL alone. Calculate QRISK3 score to guide statin therapy decision.

items.name: LDL Cholesterol
items.ref: <3.0 mmol/L general  |  <1.8 very high risk  |  <1.4 ASCVD
items.tip: LDL is the PRIMARY treatment target for cardiovascular risk reduction. Goal depends on risk category. ASCVD/very high risk: LDL <1.4 mmol/L. Primary prevention high risk: <1.8. For FH: genetic testing referral, cascade screening family members.

items.name: HDL Cholesterol
items.tip: HDL is PROTECTIVE — higher is better. Low HDL + high triglycerides = atherogenic dyslipidaemia (classic metabolic syndrome pattern). HDL <1.0 = significant cardiovascular risk. Raising HDL alone (niacin) did not reduce CV events in trials — focus on lowering LDL and treating modifiable risk factors.

items.name: Triglycerides
items.tip: TG >10 mmol/L = risk of acute pancreatitis — start fibrates urgently. TG >5.6 = secondary causes must be excluded (DM, hypothyroidism, alcohol, drugs). Fasting sample essential — TG rises significantly after a meal. In metabolic syndrome: high TG + low HDL + central obesity + insulin resistance.

items.name: Non-HDL Cholesterol
items.ref: <4.0 mmol/L general  |  <2.6 high risk  |  <2.2 very high risk
items.tip: Non-HDL = Total cholesterol − HDL. Includes VLDL, IDL, LDL — all atherogenic particles. Better predictor of CV events than LDL alone, especially in diabetics and hypertriglyceridaemia. Now preferred treatment target in ESC/EAS guidelines.

items.name: Lipoprotein(a) — Lp(a)
dn: Specific Lp(a)-lowering agents (inclisiran, pelacarsen — in trials)
items.tip: Lp(a) is an independent, inherited CV risk factor. Measure ONCE in lifetime (genetic — doesn't change with lifestyle). High Lp(a) >50 mg/dL = significantly elevated CV risk — consider more aggressive LDL lowering. Statins may slightly increase Lp(a) — important in FH patients.

items.name: Fasting Plasma Glucose
items.tip: Two abnormal fasting readings = diabetes (if asymptomatic). One reading + symptoms = sufficient for diagnosis. FPG 6.1–6.9 = impaired fasting glucose (IFG) — intermediate hyperglycaemia, high risk of progression to T2DM. Lifestyle intervention reduces progression by 58%.

items.name: HbA1c
up: Iron deficiency anaemia (falsely elevated — delayed RBC turnover)
items.tip: HbA1c reflects average glucose over past 2–3 months (RBC lifespan). UNRELIABLE in: haemoglobinopathy, haemolysis, pregnancy, CKD — use fasting glucose or 2-hr OGTT instead. Treatment targets: T1DM = 48–53 mmol/mol. T2DM = 48–58 mmol/mol (individualise — tighter if young, looser if elderly/frail).

items.name: 2-hr OGTT (75g Oral Glucose Tolerance Test)
items.tip: Gold standard for gestational diabetes screening (GDM). Also used to diagnose IGT when other tests borderline. Patient must eat carbohydrates for 3 days before test. In pregnancy: any GDM = strict glucose targets to prevent macrosomia and complications.

items.name: Random Plasma Glucose
items.tip: Random glucose ≥11.1 + symptoms (polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss) = diabetes diagnosed without further testing. Random glucose ≥11.1 without symptoms = repeat test needed. Critical values: <2.2 mmol/L = neuroglycopenic symptoms likely; treat urgently.

items.name: Serum Insulin & C-peptide
up: Sulphonylurea abuse: high insulin + high C-peptide
Exogenous insulin injection: high insulin + LOW C-peptide (diagnostic)
dn: Type 1 DM: very low/absent C-peptide (destroyed beta cells)
items.tip: C-peptide differentiates exogenous insulin abuse (low C-peptide) from insulinoma (high C-peptide). In T1DM: C-peptide confirms beta cell failure — useful when diagnosis uncertain (LADA vs T2DM). During hypoglycaemia: collect serum insulin + C-peptide + glucose simultaneously (Whipple's triad).

items.name: Beta-hydroxybutyrate (BHB)
items.tip: BHB is preferred over urine ketones in DKA monitoring (more accurate, faster, bedside). DKA resolution criteria: BHB <0.6 mmol/L + glucose <14 + HCO₃ >18. Euglycaemic DKA (SGLT2i): BHB elevated but glucose near-normal — easy to miss, high index of suspicion needed.

items.name: Glucose Monitoring — Targets
up: Hyperglycaemia: poor compliance, illness, steroids, intercurrent infection, under-insulinisation
dn: Hypoglycaemia: <4.0 mmol/L = 'four is the floor' — treat. <3.0 = severe. <2.5 = neuroglycopenia
items.tip: Hypoglycaemia treatment: if conscious = 15–20g fast-acting carb (glucose gel, orange juice, Lucozade) → recheck at 15 mins → repeat if still <4. If unconscious = IV dextrose (10% preferred over 50%) or glucagon IM. In hospital: always identify and treat the CAUSE.

items.name: Serum Calcium (Corrected)
items.tip: ALWAYS correct: Adjusted Ca = measured Ca + 0.02 × (40 − albumin g/L). Symptoms: Bones (pain/fractures), Groans (nausea/constipation), Stones (renal calculi), Psychic moans (depression/confusion/coma). Ca >3.5 = hypercalcaemic crisis: aggressive IV rehydration + IV zoledronic acid. Check PTH to differentiate cause.

items.name: Serum Phosphate
items.tip: Hyperphosphataemia in CKD drives secondary hyperparathyroidism and vascular calcification. Treat with dietary restriction + phosphate binders (sevelamer, calcium carbonate, lanthanum). Hypophosphataemia <0.3 mmol/L = severe — cardiac and respiratory muscle weakness risk.

items.name: PTH (Parathyroid Hormone)
up: Secondary hyperparathyroidism: CKD ★, Vit D def, hypocalcaemia (Ca low, PTH ↑)
Tertiary hyperparathyroidism (autonomous PTH after prolonged 2ary HPT)
dn: Sarcoidosis / Vit D toxicity (PTH suppressed by high Ca)
items.tip: KEY interpretation: Ca ↑ + PTH ↑ (or inappropriately normal) = PRIMARY hyperparathyroidism. Ca ↑ + PTH ↓ = malignancy or Vit D toxicity. Ca ↓ + PTH ↑ = secondary HPT (CKD/Vit D def). Ca ↓ + PTH ↓ = hypoparathyroidism. PTH independent of Ca = autonomous (tertiary HPT).

items.name: Vitamin D (25-OH Vitamin D)
dn: Vitamin D deficiency ★ (extremely common — especially South Asia, UK, elderly, veiled)
items.tip: 25-OH Vit D is the storage form — measure this. 1,25-OH (calcitriol) is active form — measure in sarcoid/granulomatous disease (produced ectopically). Deficiency: rickets (children), osteomalacia (adults), proximal myopathy, fatigue, low mood. Replace with colecalciferol (D3). Target 75–100 nmol/L.

items.name: ALP — Bone Fraction
up: Paget's disease of bone ★★★ (very high ALP, normal GGT)
items.tip: Paget's disease: ALP can be extremely elevated (>1000 U/L) with completely normal GGT — characteristic pattern. Treatment with bisphosphonates normalises ALP. Bone ALP (isoenzyme) can separate bone from liver source if needed. In osteomalacia: ALP ↑ + Vit D ↓ + low phosphate.

items.name: DEXA Scan — T-score (Bone Density)
dn: Osteoporosis: postmenopausal women ★, age >70, corticosteroids, hypogonadism, malabsorption, anorexia nervosa, rheumatoid arthritis
Osteopenia: earlier stage, fragility fracture risk increasing
items.tip: T-score compares to young healthy adult peak bone mass. Z-score compares to age-matched peers (use in premenopausal women and men <50). FRAX tool: integrates clinical risk factors + BMD to calculate 10-year fracture risk. Treat if FRAX 10-yr hip fracture risk ≥3% or major osteoporotic fracture ≥20%.

items.name: CSF Opening Pressure
up: Space-occupying lesion (LP CONTRAINDICATED if raised ICP with SOL)
items.tip: Always perform CT head before LP if: papilloedema, focal neurology, GCS <13, immunocompromised, new-onset seizures. IIH: young obese woman, headache, papilloedema, normal MRI — opening pressure >25 cmH₂O diagnostic. Measure in LATERAL DECUBITUS position.

items.name: CSF Protein
up: TB meningitis (1–5 g/L, can be very high)
Guillain-Barré syndrome (very high protein, albumino-cytological dissociation)
items.tip: Protein elevated in virtually all serious CNS infections. Albumino-cytological dissociation = high protein + normal cell count = Guillain-Barré ★. Very high protein + sticky/xanthochromic CSF = TB meningitis until proven otherwise. Always pair with cell count and glucose.

items.name: CSF Glucose
items.abbr: CSF Glucose — always compare to serum
up: Diabetes mellitus (hyperglycaemia raises CSF glucose proportionally)
dn: Bacterial meningitis ★★★ (<0.4 ratio = strongly suggests bacterial)
items.tip: ALWAYS take serum glucose simultaneously. Low CSF glucose = bacteria or TB consuming glucose. Ratio <0.4 = very strong indicator of bacterial meningitis. Normal ratio with high WBC = viral meningitis. Low glucose + high protein + lymphocytes = TB until proven otherwise.

items.name: CSF Cell Count
up: Lymphocytes 10–500: Viral meningitis ★, TB, fungal, aseptic
Eosinophils: parasitic meningitis (angiostrongyliasis), fungal, shunt infection
Mixed: early bacterial, partially treated, listeria, TB
items.tip: Cell type is THE KEY distinguisher: neutrophils = bacterial (treat immediately — do NOT wait for culture). Lymphocytes = viral or TB. In bacterial meningitis: treat empirically with ceftriaxone + amoxicillin (listeria cover >60yrs, pregnant, immunocompromised) + dexamethasone.

items.name: CSF Xanthochromia & RBCs
up: Subarachnoid haemorrhage ★★★ (yellow colour from oxyhaemoglobin → bilirubin >2 hrs after bleeding)
items.tip: If CT head negative but clinical suspicion of SAH: MUST perform LP >2 hrs after symptom onset. Send for spectrophotometry (not just visual inspection). Xanthochromia persists up to 2 weeks. Distinguishing SAH from traumatic tap: SAH = uniformly bloody all tubes, xanthochromia; Traumatic tap = blood clears between tube 1 and tube 4.

items.name: PSA (Prostate-Specific Antigen)
up: Recent ejaculation / DRE / catheterisation / prostate biopsy (wait 4–6 wks)
dn: 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors (finasteride, dutasteride) halve PSA — adjust interpretation
items.tip: PSA is NOT a perfect screening test. Sensitivity ~70%, specificity ~60%. Many false positives (prostatitis, BPH) and false negatives. Free:total PSA ratio: <15% = more likely malignant. PSA velocity (rate of rise) and PSA density (PSA/prostate volume) add discriminatory value. Always confirm with MRI and biopsy before treatment decisions.

items.name: AFP (Alpha-Fetoprotein)
up: Hepatocellular carcinoma ★ (AFP >400 ng/mL = highly suggestive with liver mass on imaging)
Pregnancy (normal in maternal serum — fetal source)
Neural tube defects (elevated maternal serum AFP)
items.tip: HCC surveillance: 6-monthly AFP + ultrasound in cirrhotic patients. AFP >400 = likely HCC if liver mass present. Normal AFP does NOT exclude HCC (20% of HCC don't produce AFP). In testicular cancer: AFP never elevated in PURE seminoma — if AFP raised, treat as non-seminomatous. AFP is also tumour marker for gestational trophoblastic disease.

items.name: CEA (Carcinoembryonic Antigen)
items.tip: CEA should NOT be used for colorectal cancer screening — it has poor sensitivity for early disease. Main use: POST-RESECTION monitoring for recurrence. Rising CEA after curative resection = recurrence until proven otherwise — CT staging. CEA >10 ng/mL = likely metastatic disease. Normalises after curative surgery.

items.name: CA 19-9
up: Benign biliary disease (cholangitis, jaundice — can be very high!)
dn: Lewis antigen-negative individuals cannot produce CA 19-9 (5–10% of population — permanently zero)
items.tip: CA 19-9 is elevated by BILIARY OBSTRUCTION regardless of malignancy — uninformative with jaundice until obstruction relieved. Use to monitor known pancreatic cancer response to treatment. Pre-operatively: CA 19-9 >200 suggests unresectability. Cannot diagnose pancreatic cancer in isolation.

items.name: CA 125
items.tip: CA 125 alone is not a screening test for ovarian cancer. Use with USS in post-menopausal women with symptoms. RMI (Risk of Malignancy Index) = CA125 × USS score × menopausal status. RMI >200 = refer to gynaecological oncology. CA125 very useful for MONITORING ovarian cancer treatment response and detecting recurrence.

items.name: Beta-hCG
up: Gestational trophoblastic disease ★ (hydatidiform mole, choriocarcinoma — can be very high)
Testicular germ cell tumour ★ (seminoma and non-seminoma)
Some other cancers (liver, gastric, pancreatic — ectopic production)
items.tip: Testicular cancer staging uses AFP + β-hCG + LDH together (S-stage in TNM). Pure seminoma can produce β-hCG (never AFP). Choriocarcinoma: extremely high β-hCG, highly chemotherapy-responsive — do NOT miss. Serial β-hCG is gold standard for monitoring gestational trophoblastic disease treatment response.

items.name: CA 15-3 & CEA — Breast
items.tip: CA 15-3 is used for MONITORING metastatic breast cancer, not for diagnosis or screening. Rising CA 15-3 in known breast cancer = disease progression (confirm with imaging before changing treatment). Up to 25% of breast cancers are CA 15-3 negative. CEA also useful adjunct in breast cancer monitoring.

items.name: LDH — Haematological Malignancy
up: Lymphoma ★ (especially aggressive DLBCL — high LDH = poor prognosis, included in IPI score)
items.tip: In lymphoma: LDH is part of the International Prognostic Index (IPI). Elevated LDH = advanced disease, worse prognosis. Serial LDH used to monitor treatment response — falling LDH = responding. In haematology: LDH >2× ULN = high-risk disease marker. Also critical in tumour lysis syndrome monitoring.

items.name: Tumour Markers — Combined Use
up: AFP + β-hCG + LDH together = TNM S-staging for testicular GCT
AFP elevated = non-seminomatous component (even if histology shows pure seminoma)
β-hCG elevated: can occur in pure seminoma
Very high markers = poor prognosis group (IGCCCG classification)
items.tip: MUST measure AFP, β-hCG, and LDH BEFORE orchiectomy (staging purpose) and AFTER (half-life assessment). AFP half-life = 5–7 days. β-hCG half-life = 24–36 hrs. Markers not falling appropriately post-op = residual/metastatic disease. Rising markers after completion of chemo = relapse.

items.name: pH
up: Respiratory alkalosis (hyperventilation, anxiety, PE, high altitude)
dn: Metabolic acidosis (DKA ★, lactic acidosis, renal failure, diarrhoea)
items.tip: pH <7.35 = acidaemia. pH >7.45 = alkalaemia. Normal pH does NOT mean normal — compensation can mask mixed disorders. Always interpret pH with PaCO₂ and HCO₃ together. Step 1: Is it acidosis or alkalosis? Step 2: Primary respiratory or metabolic? Step 3: Is compensation adequate?

items.name: PaCO₂
up: Respiratory acidosis — hypoventilation ★ (COPD, sedation, obesity hypoventilation)
dn: Respiratory alkalosis — hyperventilation ★ (anxiety, pain, PE, pregnancy, mechanical overventilation)
items.tip: PaCO₂ = respiratory component. ↑ PaCO₂ + ↓ pH = respiratory acidosis. ↓ PaCO₂ + ↑ pH = respiratory alkalosis. Winter's formula for expected compensation in metabolic acidosis: PaCO₂ = (1.5 × HCO₃) + 8 ± 2. If actual PaCO₂ higher than expected = also has respiratory acidosis.

items.name: PaO₂
dn: Type 1 RF: PaO₂ <8 kPa + normal/low PaCO₂ (V/Q mismatch — pneumonia, PE, pulmonary oedema, ARDS)
Type 2 RF: PaO₂ <8 kPa + PaCO₂ >6 kPa (hypoventilation — COPD, neuromuscular, obesity)
items.tip: P/F ratio (PaO₂/FiO₂): >400 = normal. 200–400 = mild ARDS. 100–200 = moderate ARDS. <100 = severe ARDS. A-a gradient = PAO₂ − PaO₂. Normal A-a = Age/4 + 4 mmHg. Elevated A-a gradient = V/Q mismatch or shunt (not pure hypoventilation). Type 1 RF target SpO₂ 94–98%. Type 2 RF (COPD): target 88–92% (hypoxic drive).

items.name: HCO₃ (Bicarbonate)
up: Respiratory acidosis compensation (chronic CO₂ retention — renal HCO₃ retention)
dn: Metabolic acidosis ★ (DKA, lactic acidosis, diarrhoea, RTA, uraemia, poisoning)
items.tip: Anion gap (AG) = Na − (Cl + HCO₃). Normal = 8–12 mmol/L. High AG metabolic acidosis mnemonic MUDPILES: Methanol, Uraemia, DKA ★, Propylene glycol, Isoniazid/Iron, Lactic acidosis ★, Ethylene glycol, Salicylates. Normal AG (hyperchloraemic) acidosis: diarrhoea ★, RTA, saline overload, acetazolamide.

items.name: Base Excess (BE) & Lactate
up: Lactate: sepsis/shock ★★, liver failure, metformin toxicity, ischaemia, strenuous exercise
items.tip: Base deficit <−6 in trauma = significant shock — predicts need for massive transfusion. Lactate ≥2 = hyperlactataemia (monitor, investigate cause). Lactate ≥4 = septic shock criterion → 30mL/kg IV fluid + antibiotics within 1 hour (Surviving Sepsis Campaign). Serial lactate clearance (≥10% decrease/2h) guides resuscitation. Persistent high lactate despite fluids = vasopressors + ICU.

items.name: PT / INR
up: Warfarin therapy ★ (target INR 2–3 for AF/DVT; 2.5–3.5 for mechanical valve)
Liver disease ★ (↓ synthesis factors II, VII, IX, X — VII first to fall, shortest half-life)
dn: Hypercoagulable states (not clinically useful as screen)
items.tip: INR monitors extrinsic pathway. INR >1.5 = significant coagulopathy. Warfarin reversal: INR 3–5 (no bleed) = hold dose. INR >5 or bleeding = Vitamin K IV + PCC (4-factor) preferred over FFP. Do NOT give FFP routinely for elevated INR — only if active bleeding or pre-procedure. DOAC patients: INR is UNRELIABLE — do not monitor warfarin with DOAC.

items.name: APTT
up: Haemophilia A (Factor VIII ↓) ★ or B (Factor IX ↓)
Lupus anticoagulant ★ (paradox — prolonged APTT but THROMBOSIS risk)
items.tip: APTT monitors intrinsic pathway. Lupus anticoagulant: APTT prolonged but causes THROMBOSIS not bleeding — mixing study fails to correct (inhibitor). Confirm with DRVVT. LMWH does NOT prolong APTT — monitor with anti-Xa. Haemophilia: isolated prolonged APTT + normal PT + normal bleeding time → factor assay (VIII or IX).

items.name: D-Dimer
up: Post-surgery, pregnancy, malignancy, sepsis, MI, stroke — all elevate D-dimer
items.tip: D-dimer = RULE-OUT test. Negative D-dimer + low pre-test probability (Wells score) = DVT/PE safely excluded. Age-adjusted cut-off (age × 0.01 µg/mL in >50y) reduces unnecessary CT-PA by 30%. NEVER use D-dimer to diagnose PE — it cannot. Markedly elevated D-dimer (>5) with clinical features = DIC screen: fibrinogen, platelets, PT, APTT, blood film.

items.name: Fibrinogen & TT
up: Fibrinogen: acute phase response ★, pregnancy, malignancy, post-surgery
TT prolonged: heparin contamination ★ (most common), fibrinogen deficiency, DIC, dabigatran
dn: Reptilase time normal + TT prolonged = heparin effect (reptilase not affected by heparin)
items.tip: Fibrinogen <1.5 g/L = give cryoprecipitate (10 units raises fibrinogen ~1 g/L). Fibrinogen <1.0 g/L = emergency cryoprecipitate. In obstetric haemorrhage: fibrinogen <2.0 = early predictor of severity. DIC diagnosis: ↓ fibrinogen + ↑ D-dimer + ↑ PT + ↑ APTT + ↓ platelets. TT used to detect: heparin contamination, fibrinogen abnormality, dabigatran effect.

items.name: Anti-Xa Level
items.tip: Anti-Xa is the CORRECT monitoring test for LMWH (not APTT). Monitor in: extremes of weight (<45kg or >100kg), renal impairment (CrCl <30), pregnancy, neonates. Prophylactic LMWH target: 0.2–0.4 IU/mL. Treatment: 0.6–1.0 IU/mL at 4h post-dose. For UFH infusions: target anti-Xa 0.3–0.7 IU/mL OR APTT ratio 1.5–2.5.

items.name: High-Sensitivity Troponin (hs-cTn)
items.ref: hs-cTnI <26 ng/L male, <16 ng/L female | hs-cTnT <14 ng/L
up: Type 2 MI (demand ischaemia from PE, sepsis, tachyarrhythmia)
Renal failure (↓ clearance — persistent low elevation)
dn: Very low hs-cTn — MI unlikely if combined with low pre-test probability
items.tip: 0h/1h algorithm: if hs-cTnT <5 ng/L at 0h AND delta <3 ng/L at 1h = 99.6% NPV for NSTEMI (rule-out). Delta (absolute rise) is MORE important than single value. Peak troponin 12–24h post-MI. Renal failure: use relative delta >20% rather than absolute. Troponin elevation without ACS = 'non-ischaemic myocardial injury' — requires clinical context + ECG + echo.

items.name: BNP / NT-proBNP
items.ref: BNP <35 pg/mL (HF unlikely) | NT-proBNP <125 pg/mL (<75y), <300 pg/mL (>75y)
up: Heart failure ★★★ (primary indication — both systolic and diastolic)
dn: Obesity (↓ production despite HF — can miss HF in obese patients)
items.tip: NT-proBNP rule-in by age: <50y = 450 pg/mL. 50–75y = 900 pg/mL. >75y = 1800 pg/mL. Negative BNP/NT-proBNP has high NPV for HF (~95% — useful to exclude in acute dyspnoea). Obese patients: lower NT-proBNP despite significant HF. Trend is more useful than single value for monitoring treatment response. Persistently elevated despite treatment = poor 12-month prognosis.

items.name: CK-MB & LDH
items.ref: CK-MB <25 U/L (<5% total CK) | LDH 135–225 U/L
up: CK-MB: MI (rises 4–6h, peaks 12–24h, returns normal 48–72h), cardiac surgery, myocarditis
LDH: MI (late — rises 24–48h, peaks 3–6 days), haemolysis ★, lymphoma, liver disease, PE
items.tip: CK-MB still useful for: (1) Detecting re-infarction — returns to normal faster than troponin, so new CK-MB rise after normalisation = re-MI. (2) Estimating infarct size (peak correlates with area of necrosis). LDH1 > LDH2 (flipped pattern) = cardiac or haemolytic source. Very high LDH (>1000 U/L) in haematological malignancy correlates with tumour burden. LDH >500 in PCP pneumonia is characteristic.

items.name: ANA (Antinuclear Antibody)
up: SLE ★★★ (95–99% sensitive — high sensitivity screening test)
Sjögren syndrome, scleroderma, MCTD, drug-induced lupus
dn: Negative ANA makes SLE very unlikely — high NPV
items.tip: ANA is a SCREENING test only. Positive ANA → ENA panel: anti-dsDNA (SLE specific ★), anti-Sm (SLE specific), anti-Ro/La (Sjögren, neonatal lupus), anti-Scl-70 (scleroderma), anti-centromere (CREST), anti-Jo-1 (myositis/ILD). ANA pattern: homogeneous = dsDNA/histone. Speckled = Sm/RNP/Ro/La. Centromere = CREST syndrome.

items.name: Anti-dsDNA & Anti-Sm
up: SLE ★★★ (anti-dsDNA: 70% sensitivity, 97% specificity. Anti-Sm: 25–30% sensitivity, 99% specificity)
Anti-dsDNA titres correlate with SLE disease activity — rising = flare, especially nephritis ★
dn: Negative anti-dsDNA — less likely active lupus nephritis
items.tip: Anti-dsDNA is the MOST USEFUL antibody in SLE monitoring. Serial titres + C3/C4 guide flare prediction. Anti-dsDNA + ↓ complement + active urine sediment = lupus nephritis → kidney biopsy urgently. Anti-Sm is highly specific for SLE (99%) but insensitive — if positive, diagnoses SLE even without meeting full criteria.

items.name: Anti-TPO (Anti-Thyroid Peroxidase) ★
up: Non-thyroidal autoimmune disease (T1DM, Addison's, pernicious anaemia — associated)
10% healthy euthyroid women (especially >45y) — low-titre, monitor TSH annually
items.tip: Anti-TPO confirms AUTOIMMUNE thyroid aetiology. Positive anti-TPO in euthyroid patient = 4× increased hypothyroidism risk per decade — recheck TSH annually. In pregnancy: positive anti-TPO + TSH >2.5 in T1 = treat with levothyroxine (reduces miscarriage and postpartum thyroiditis risk). Anti-TPO does NOT correlate with severity — it confirms the diagnosis. All patients with hypothyroidism should have anti-TPO checked at diagnosis.

items.name: Anti-TG & TRAb
up: Anti-TG: Hashimoto's (60–70%), Graves' (50%), thyroid cancer follow-up interference ★
TRAb (TSH receptor Ab): Graves' disease ★★★ (95%), neonatal thyrotoxicosis
dn: Negative TRAb effectively rules out Graves' disease
items.tip: Anti-TG critical in thyroid cancer: elevated anti-TG falsely LOWERS serum thyroglobulin (Tg) — making cancer recurrence monitoring unreliable. Always check anti-TG alongside Tg post-thyroidectomy. If anti-TG positive → Tg is unreliable → use imaging (neck USS + whole body scan) instead. TRAb in pregnancy: check at 32–34 weeks in Graves — high TRAb predicts neonatal thyrotoxicosis (plan paediatric review at delivery).

items.name: ANCA (cANCA / pANCA)
up: p-ANCA non-specific: IBD (60% UC), autoimmune hepatitis, drug-induced vasculitis
dn: Negative ANCA makes AAV unlikely (but possible in limited GPA)
items.tip: ANCA-associated vasculitis (AAV) triad: small vessel vasculitis + ANCA + end-organ damage. Renal emergency: haematuria + proteinuria + crescentic GN + positive ANCA → immunosuppression immediately (rituximab or cyclophosphamide + IV methylprednisolone). Pulmonary-renal syndrome (haemoptysis + GN) = ANCA, anti-GBM, or SLE. Request anti-GBM (Goodpasture) alongside ANCA in pulmonary-renal presentations.

items.name: RF & Anti-CCP
up: RF also in: Sjögren ★, hepatitis C ★, cryoglobulinaemia, SBE, sarcoidosis, ageing
Anti-CCP: can be positive YEARS before clinical arthritis (pre-RA)
dn: Seronegative RA (20–30% RF/anti-CCP negative — still diagnose clinically + DAS28)
items.tip: Anti-CCP (anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide) is the most specific test for RA (98%). Anti-CCP + inflammatory polyarthritis = RA until proven otherwise → refer rheumatology, start DMARDs early. High anti-CCP predicts erosive, severe RA — justifies aggressive treatment. RF is non-specific — always pair with anti-CCP. Combined RF + anti-CCP positive = highest specificity for RA (>99%).

items.name: Complement C3 & C4
up: Acute phase response — C3 and C4 both rise in infection/inflammation
dn: SLE ★★★ (C3 + C4 both fall — immune complex consumption of classic pathway)
MPGN type II — C3↓, C4 normal (alternative pathway activation via C3 nephritic factor)
Hereditary angioedema — C4 markedly low (C1-inhibitor deficiency)
items.tip: Complement pattern interpretation: C3↓ + C4↓ = classic pathway (SLE ★, lupus nephritis, cryoglobulinaemia). C3↓ + C4 normal = alternative pathway (MPGN II, C3 nephritic factor). C4↓ + C3 normal = C1-inhibitor deficiency (hereditary angioedema) or isolated C4 deficiency. Serial C3/C4 useful in SLE monitoring: rising = remission, falling = flare.

items.name: Antiphospholipid Antibodies (APLA)
up: APS (antiphospholipid syndrome): arterial/venous thrombosis + pregnancy morbidity + positive APLA ★
Transient positivity: infections, drugs — NOT diagnostic
items.tip: APS diagnosis requires: (1) Clinical: thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity (3+ miscarriages <10wk, 1+ loss >10wk, premature birth <34wk for pre-eclampsia). (2) Lab: positive on 2 occasions ≥12 weeks apart — lupus anticoagulant (LA), anti-cardiolipin IgG/IgM, anti-β₂GP1 IgG/IgM. Triple positivity = highest thrombosis risk. Treatment: warfarin (target INR 2–3) — DOACs INFERIOR to warfarin in APS (rivaroxaban non-inferior for venous only, but NOT for arterial — avoid in arterial APS).

items.name: Cortisol & ACTH
up: Cortisol ↑: Cushing's ★ (↑ + loss of diurnal rhythm), stress/acute illness, pseudo-Cushing (obesity, depression, alcohol)
ACTH ↑: Cushing's disease (pituitary) ★, ectopic ACTH (SCLC ★ — very high ACTH), Addison's (↑ ACTH compensatory with ↓ cortisol)
dn: ACTH ↓: adrenal adenoma/carcinoma (autonomous cortisol suppresses ACTH), secondary adrenal insufficiency
items.tip: Cushing's screening: 1mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test (normal = AM cortisol <50 nmol/L). OR 24h urine free cortisol. OR late-night salivary cortisol (best test — loss of diurnal nadir). Adrenal insufficiency: AM cortisol <100 nmol/L = likely. 100–500 = inconclusive → Synacthen test (SST): cortisol should rise to >550 nmol/L at 30 min. ACTH localisation: ↑ ACTH = ACTH-dependent (pituitary or ectopic). ↓ ACTH = ACTH-independent (adrenal).

items.name: TSH (Thyroid Stimulating Hormone)
dn: Secondary hypothyroidism (↓ TSH + ↓ FT4 — pituitary failure)
items.tip: TSH = most sensitive thyroid test. Start with TSH alone. If abnormal → add FT4 (+ FT3 if TSH suppressed). TSH 4–10 mIU/L (subclinical hypothyroid): treat if symptomatic, anti-TPO positive, or pregnant. TSH >10 = treat regardless. Amiodarone: causes hypothyroidism (most common) OR hyperthyroidism — monitor TFTs every 6 months. In pregnancy: TSH >2.5 in T1 → start levothyroxine 25–50 µg/day.

items.name: Prolactin
up: Dopamine antagonists ★★ (metoclopramide, domperidone, haloperidol, risperidone, methyldopa)
items.tip: PRL >5000 mIU/L = prolactinoma very likely. PRL 1000–5000 = could be drug-induced, hypothyroidism, or macroprolactinaemia. Always exclude: pregnancy + hypothyroidism + medications FIRST. Macroprolactinaemia: PEG precipitation test — >60% precipitates = macroprolactin (clinically insignificant — avoid treatment). Symptomatic prolactinoma: cabergoline 0.5mg twice weekly → highly effective (>90% normalise PRL).

items.name: LH / FSH & Testosterone
items.ref: LH: follicular 2–15 IU/L | FSH: follicular 3–10 IU/L | Total T: male 9.9–27.8 nmol/L, female 0.4–2.0 nmol/L
up: LH + FSH ↑: POI/menopause (↑ FSH + ↑ LH + ↓ oestrogen), primary testicular failure (Klinefelter's ★), post-gonadectomy
Testosterone ↑: female PCOS ★ (mild), adrenal/ovarian tumour (very high), anabolic steroids, testicular germ cell tumour
dn: LH + FSH ↓: hypothalamic amenorrhoea (stress, low BMI, over-exercise), hyperprolactinaemia, Kallmann syndrome, pituitary failure
Testosterone ↓: primary hypogonadism (Klinefelter's), secondary hypogonadism (pituitary), obesity (↓ SHBG)
items.tip: FSH >40 IU/L + amenorrhoea ≥12 months in <40y = POI — HRT essential (osteoporosis + cardiovascular protection). LH:FSH >2:1 in PCOS (60% of cases — not a diagnostic criterion). Morning testosterone (7–10 AM) essential — diurnal variation. Free testosterone (calculated) more relevant in obese (↓ SHBG → falsely low total T). Female total T >5 nmol/L → investigate virilising cause.

items.name: IGF-1 & Growth Hormone
items.ref: IGF-1: age-dependent (see lab reference) | GH suppressed <1 µg/L post-OGTT
up: IGF-1 ↑: acromegaly ★★★ (best screening test), puberty (normal), obesity (mildly elevated)
GH fails to suppress (<1 µg/L) in acromegaly on OGTT — confirmatory test
dn: IGF-1 ↓: GH deficiency ★ (pituitary failure), malnutrition, liver disease, hypothyroidism, anorexia
items.tip: IGF-1 is BEST screening test for acromegaly (stable throughout day, unlike GH which is pulsatile). Elevated IGF-1 → confirm with OGTT (GH measured at 0, 30, 60, 90, 120 min — normal = suppression to <1 µg/L at 2h). Acromegaly features: enlarged hands/feet, macroglossia, prognathism, carpal tunnel, sleep apnoea, HTN, T2DM, colonic polyps (3× colorectal cancer risk), organomegaly. Treatment: transsphenoidal surgery (first line) → somatostatin analogues (octreotide) if incomplete.

items.name: Digoxin
up: Toxicity ★: nausea/vomiting, yellow-green visual haloes, bradyarrhythmias, AV block, bidirectional VT
Toxicity enhanced by: hypokalaemia ★★, hypomagnesaemia, hypercalcaemia, hypothyroidism, renal failure, amiodarone, verapamil, clarithromycin (all raise digoxin levels)
dn: Sub-therapeutic — inadequate rate control or antiarrhythmic effect
items.tip: ECG of digoxin effect (not toxicity): reversed tick ST depression (scooping), short QT. Toxicity ECG: bradycardia, AV block, any arrhythmia. Treatment: stop digoxin, correct K⁺ to >4.0 mmol/L, anti-digoxin Fab (DigiFab/Digibind) for life-threatening toxicity. Narrow therapeutic index — always check K⁺ with every digoxin level. Absolutely avoid in WPW.

items.name: Lithium
up: Toxicity ★: tremor (first sign), GI upset, polyuria/polydipsia, confusion, ataxia, seizures, arrhythmias
Toxicity precipitants: NSAIDs ★★★ (double levels), ACEi/ARBs ★★★, thiazide diuretics, dehydration, low-sodium diet
dn: Sub-therapeutic — inadequate mood stabilisation or prophylaxis
items.tip: Narrowest therapeutic window of all psychiatric drugs. Monitor pre-treatment (U&E, TFTs, ECG, pregnancy test) then every 6 months. NSAIDs are the most common cause of iatrogenic lithium toxicity — educate every patient. Dehydration (diarrhoea, vomiting, hot weather, ramadan fasting) → dangerously elevated levels. Dialysis for level >3.5 or severe neurological toxicity. Never stop lithium abruptly — taper over 4 weeks (rebound mania risk).

items.name: Vancomycin
up: Red man syndrome: flushing/rash during infusion — slow rate (not true allergy)
items.tip: AUC/MIC monitoring now preferred over trough-only (reduces toxicity). Target AUC 400–600 for serious MRSA. Monitor creatinine daily during therapy. In renal impairment: extend dosing interval (not reduce dose). High-risk nephrotoxicity: combination with pip-tazo ★ — avoid where possible or closely monitor. Check level after 3rd dose in stable renal function. Load with 25–30 mg/kg for serious infections.

items.name: Phenytoin & Valproate
items.ref: Phenytoin: 40–80 µmol/L (10–20 mg/L) | Valproate: 350–700 µmol/L (50–100 mg/L) | Both: pre-dose trough
up: Phenytoin toxicity: nystagmus ★ (first sign) → ataxia → dysarthria → drowsiness
Valproate toxicity: hepatotoxicity ★, hyperammonaemia (even with normal LFTs), pancreatitis, teratogenicity ★★★
items.tip: Phenytoin: non-linear kinetics — small dose ↑ causes disproportionately large level rise. In hypoalbuminaemia: corrected phenytoin = measured ÷ [(albumin g/dL × 0.1) + 0.1]. Major interactions: induces CYP450 → reduces warfarin, OCP, statins. Valproate: ABSOLUTELY contraindicated in women of childbearing potential (teratogenicity — neural tube defects + neurodevelopmental harm). MHRA: annual PPP review required for all women on valproate. Valproate-induced encephalopathy: measure ammonia — may be elevated with normal LFTs.

items.name: Gentamicin & Aminoglycosides
items.ref: Once-daily dosing: pre-dose trough <1 mg/L | Peak (1h post): 5–10 mg/L
up: Nephrotoxicity ★ (proximal tubule accumulation — monitor creatinine daily)
Ototoxicity (vestibular > cochlear — irreversible at high levels)
dn: Sub-therapeutic — inadequate gram-negative or endocarditis coverage
items.tip: Once-daily gentamicin: 5–7 mg/kg/day (Hartford nomogram for renal dosing). Pre-dose trough MUST be <1 mg/L before next dose (drug-free interval prevents nephrotoxicity). Monitor creatinine DAILY. Stop if creatinine rises >30% from baseline. Max duration: 5–7 days where possible. Combination nephrotoxicity with: vancomycin, NSAIDs, amphotericin, furosemide — avoid or monitor closely. Endocarditis (synergistic low-dose): trough <0.5 mg/L, peak 3–5 mg/L.

items.name: Critical Values — Haematology
items.ref: Values requiring IMMEDIATE physician notification and action
dn: Hb <60 g/L — severe anaemia → consider transfusion (threshold contextual)
Neutrophils <0.5 × 10⁹/L ★ — severe neutropenia → reverse barrier nursing, consider G-CSF
Platelets <20 × 10⁹/L — spontaneous bleeding risk → transfuse if <10 (or <20 if febrile/bleeding)
items.tip: Laboratory critical values: call ordering physician within 60 minutes. Document: who notified, time, read-back of result. Patient must be reviewed within 1 hour. Do NOT simply result and walk away. Repeat sample if clinically discordant — haemolysis or sample error is common cause of spurious hyperkalaemia or thrombocytopenia.

items.name: Critical Values — Biochemistry
items.ref: Values requiring IMMEDIATE physician notification and action
up: Na >160 mmol/L — severe hypernatraemia — brain shrinkage, coma
K >6.5 mmol/L ★★★ — CARDIAC ARREST RISK — treat immediately
Corrected Ca >3.5 mmol/L — hypercalcaemic crisis — confusion, coma, renal failure
Creatinine >800 µmol/L — acute severe renal failure → dialysis consideration
Ammonia >150 µmol/L — hepatic encephalopathy grade III/IV
dn: Na <120 mmol/L ★★★ — severe hyponatraemia — seizure risk — correct ≤10 mmol/L/24h (osmotic demyelination risk if faster)
K <2.5 mmol/L — severe hypokalaemia — ventricular arrhythmia
Corrected Ca <1.75 mmol/L — severe hypocalcaemia — tetany, laryngospasm, seizures
Glucose <2.5 mmol/L — neuroglycopenia — coma, permanent brain damage
Mg <0.5 mmol/L — refractory hypokalaemia, arrhythmia, respiratory failure
Phosphate <0.3 mmol/L — refeeding syndrome — haemolysis, respiratory failure
items.tip: Hyperkalaemia emergency (K >6.5): Step 1 — IV calcium gluconate 10mL 10% over 10 min (membrane stabilisation, IMMEDIATE). Step 2 — IV insulin 10U + 50mL 50% dextrose (shifts K into cells, onset 20 min). Step 3 — Salbutamol 10mg nebulised (onset 30 min). Step 4 — Resonium/patiromer (removes K, hours). Step 5 — Dialysis if refractory. ECG monitoring throughout. Identify and treat cause.

items.name: Critical Values — Microbiology
items.ref: Positive results requiring urgent clinical action and documentation
up: Positive blood culture — ALWAYS call immediately — specify organism and sensitivities
CSF any organism — meningitis — antibiotics within 30 minutes of decision
MRSA in sterile site — contact precautions + notify infection control
C. difficile toxin positive — contact precautions immediately + stop offending antibiotics
AFB smear positive (TB) — respiratory isolation + public health notification
HIV positive (new diagnosis) — urgent linkage to HIV care
items.tip: Blood culture Gram stain guide: Gram-positive cocci clusters = MRSA/MSSA → vancomycin (MRSA) or flucloxacillin (MSSA). Gram-positive cocci chains = Streptococcus/Enterococcus → benzylpenicillin or amoxicillin. Gram-negative rods = Enterobacteriaceae or Pseudomonas → pip-tazo or cefuroxime. Yeast = Candida → echinocandin (ICU). Staph. aureus bacteraemia: ALWAYS requires echocardiogram and minimum 14 days IV antibiotics (28 days if deep focus).
